fact: Around a hundred thousand civilians were pushed to the waterfront during the fall of Smyrna in September 1922
source: Paradise Lost: Smyrna, 1922, Giles Milton (2008). Estimates of refugees on the quay range from 100,000 to over 200,000.

fact: Allied warships were present in Smyrna's harbor but had orders not to intervene, partly due to geopolitical interests including oil concessions
source: Ionian Vision: Greece in Asia Minor, 1919–1922, Michael Llewellyn Smith (1973/1998). Also discussed in Marjorie Housepian Dobkin, Smyrna 1922: The Destruction of a City.

fact: The fire began in the Armenian quarter of Smyrna
source: Smyrna 1922: The Destruction of a City, Marjorie Housepian Dobkin (1971/1988). Multiple eyewitness accounts place the fire's origin in the Armenian quarter on September 13, 1922.

fact: Turkish soldiers set the fire (as opposed to it being accidental or set by minorities)
source: Widely attributed by eyewitnesses and most modern historians, though Turkey disputes this. See Paradise Lost, Giles Milton (2008), and eyewitness accounts compiled by the American consul George Horton in The Blight of Asia (1926).

fact: Allied warships refused to lower boats to rescue refugees from the waterfront
source: The Blight of Asia, George Horton (1926); also corroborated by multiple naval officers' accounts cited in Smyrna 1922, Marjorie Housepian Dobkin.

fact: Crews on the warships played music (bands/gramophones) to drown out the screams of people on the quay
source: Reported by multiple eyewitnesses. Cited in Paradise Lost, Giles Milton (2008), Chapter 25, and in Housepian Dobkin's Smyrna 1922. Often attributed to accounts of Italian and British ships.
